topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the faded ink on the brittle paper, the silence of his apartment amplifying the rustle of the old document. He had spent the last week staring at the meticulously crafted narrative of his life—the triumphs, the quiet moments, the supposed arc of his existence—and a cold, hollow ache settled deep in his chest. The words described a man he barely recognized, a collection of curated anecdotes that felt alien. He reread the passage detailing his 'defining passion,' a subject he had never pursued. A tremor ran through his hand, causing him to drop the paper onto the worn rug. The air in the room seemed to thicken, pressing down on him until breathing felt like a conscious, laborious effort. He stood up, pacing the narrow confines of the living room, his movements jerky and uncoordinated, searching for an anchor that wasn't there.

[story 2]

"It's all a lie," Sarah whispered, the sound catching in her throat like shattered glass. She held the biographical sketch, the glossy pages mocking her with their polished perfection. Every sentence felt like an intrusion, a stranger dictating the rhythm of her days. She sat on the edge of the bed, pulling her knees to her chest, the fabric of her sweater offering little comfort against the chill that had taken root in her bones. Her gaze drifted to the window, where the city lights blurred into streaks of indifferent color. A profound emptiness washed over her, a vast, echoing space where connection should have been. She pressed her palms against her eyes, trying to physically block out the reality of the text, but the words seemed to vibrate beneath her skin, insistent and inescapable.

[story 3]

Marcus sat at his kitchen table, the single overhead bulb casting harsh shadows across the desk where the manuscript lay open. He reread the section describing his early career, the description of his ambitions sounding like a performance he hadn't rehearsed. A tightness constricted his jaw, and he clenched his fists until his knuckles turned white. He pushed the chair back violently, the scrape of wood against the floor sounding deafening in the quiet space. He walked to the window, staring out at the rain-slicked street, feeling utterly disconnected from the world outside. The weight of the unearned history settled on him, a suffocating blanket that made every breath shallow and inadequate. He didn't move for a long time, simply absorbing the pervasive sense of being utterly unmoored.
